metrics.metric: Days since last confirmed cyber incident (Gulf FS)

items.text: Air raid sirens were activated multiple times in Kuwait during the coverage period as air defense systems continued to engage incoming missile and drone threats. The Ministry of Defense confirmed that explosions heard across the country resulted from successful interception operations and urged residents to follow civil defense instructions.

The Kuwait Civil Defense mobile alert system continued issuing tiered notifications. The Ministry of Interior reiterated that all explosions heard are the result of air defense interceptions and called on residents to remain calm and adhere to safety protocols.

items.bold_heading: Cumulative siren activations since conflict began: ~157
items.text: Estimated based on ~144 cumulative as of 26 March plus continued activations on 26–27 March.

items.bold_heading: Kuwait — continued missile and drone attacks (26–27 March)
items.text: Kuwait's air defense systems intercepted hostile missile and drone attacks throughout the coverage period. The Kuwait National Guard confirmed it downed multiple UAVs within its areas of responsibility as part of ongoing efforts to protect strategic sites. Explosive disposal teams responded to new reports of falling debris. No casualties reported from these interceptions. Status: confirmed.

items.bold_heading: UAE — ballistic missile debris casualties continue (26 March)
items.text: UAE air defenses intercepted a ballistic missile over Abu Dhabi; falling debris killed two people and injured three on 26 March, raising the UAE's national death toll to 11. The UAE Ministry of Defence reported that Iran has fired more than 2,100 missiles and drones at the country since the war began. Status: confirmed.

items.bold_heading: Iran's 'Tehran Toll Booth' formalized at Strait of Hormuz (26 March)
items.text: Iran sent a letter to 176 International Maritime Organization members stating that 'non-hostile vessels' may transit the Strait of Hormuz subject to IRGC vetting and payment of fees reportedly as high as $2 million per transit. At least two vessels paid fees in yuan, brokered by a Chinese maritime services intermediary. 26 vessel transits have followed a pre-approved IRGC route. Status: confirmed.

items.bold_heading: Six Gulf states + Jordan issue joint condemnation (26 March)
items.text: The UAE, Kuwait, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Jordan issued a joint statement condemning Iran's 'blatant' and 'criminal' attacks, calling on Iraq to halt attacks launched from its territory by Iran-linked armed groups, and asserting the right to self-defense under Article 51 of the UN Charter. Status: confirmed.

The Ministry of Interior continues to advise residents to maintain emergency supplies of food, water, and medications and to follow siren protocols. The U.S. Embassy in Kuwait maintains a Level 4 'Do Not Travel' advisory and remains closed since 20 March.

Seven overhead power transmission lines were damaged by interception debris on 24 March. Four of seven lines have been restored; repair crews are working around the clock on the remaining three. The Ministry of Electricity, Water and Renewable Energy activated its Emergency Alternative Recovery Plans and is monitoring desalination plants and power generation 24/7. A new $186 million infrastructure contract was signed with HEISCO to strengthen power grid capacity.

Kuwait International Airport (KWI) remains closed to all commercial passenger traffic with no reopening date announced. Terminal 1, portions of the runway, fuel depots, and the radar system remain damaged. Three conditions must be met for reopening: structural repairs, a stable security environment, and clearance from regional aviation regulators.

Limited evacuation flights continue to operate from King Fahd International Airport (Dammam, Saudi Arabia), with passengers processed at Al Khiran Mall and bussed to the Saudi border. Kuwait Airways, Jazeera Airways, and international carriers remain suspended.

The Nuwaiseeb (Kuwait–Saudi Arabia) land border remains open with extended wait times. The Abdali (Iraq) border is reported operational but conditions remain unclear due to military activity. [SINGLE SOURCE on Iraq border]

No specific reports of acute grocery shortages or panic buying identified in the past 24 hours. Cumulative supply pressures remain elevated due to airport closure, Strait of Hormuz disruption, and Kuwait Petroleum Corporation's force majeure on oil exports. [SOURCE GAP — no KUNA or Kuwait Times updates on grocery supply in the past 24 hours]

The Kuwait Government Communication Center issued an urgent public safety advisory for severe weather conditions (heavy rain and thunderstorms), urging residents to remain indoors unless necessary and to contact the emergency hotline (112) for assistance.

President Trump extended the pause on striking Iran's energy facilities by 10 days to Monday, April 6, at Iran's request, while indirect talks via Pakistan continue; Pakistan's Foreign Minister confirmed the U.S. and Iran are engaged in 'indirect talks' with a 15-point proposal under deliberation, though Iran publicly rejected it as 'maximalist' and countered with five conditions including war reparations and sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz.

Oil prices continued to climb — Brent crude rose to $108.01/bbl (+1.8%) and WTI to $94.48/bbl (+0.9%) — as Iran's rejection of direct talks and its formalized 'toll booth' regime at the Strait of Hormuz reinforced fears that disruption will persist through Q2 2026.

The six-nation Gulf + Jordan joint statement asserting the right to self-defense under Article 51 marked a significant hardening of GCC posture toward Iran; the UAE Ambassador wrote in the Wall Street Journal that 'a simple ceasefire is not enough,' signaling Gulf states may seek a broader security settlement.

Kuwait International Airport remains closed with no reopening timeline, daily missile/drone threats persist, and partial power outages continue. Staff relocation and remote working arrangements should remain in effect; overland evacuation via Saudi Arabia remains the only viable exit route.

Oil price volatility continues to intensify — Brent at $108.01/bbl, up from $106.12 the prior day. Iran's formalized 'toll booth' regime at the Strait of Hormuz (transit at ~5 vessels/day vs. pre-war ~120) is creating a new structural constraint on global energy flows, with fees reportedly reaching $2 million per vessel. Goldman Sachs' Kuwait GDP contraction forecast of 14% in 2026 remains the baseline scenario.

Physical damage to Kuwait's power and telecommunications infrastructure elevates the risk of intermittent disruptions to electronic payment processing and cross-border settlement. No specific SWIFT disruption reported, but contingency plans should remain validated given three of seven damaged power lines are still unrepaired.
sources: Assessed based on infrastructure damage reporting

items.text: Daily missile and drone attacks on Kuwait, combined with airport closure, limit evacuation options for expatriate staff. UAE debris casualties (2 killed, 3 injured on 26 March) underscore ongoing physical risk to personnel across the Gulf. Severe weather conditions in Kuwait add a compounding factor.

Iran-linked threat actors continue to target Gulf financial infrastructure. SHIELDNXT reports 'highly organized and well-resourced' threat actors conducting DDoS, APT infiltration, spear-phishing, and unauthorized access attempts against banking and financial services across Kuwait, UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar. Unit 42's March 26 update notes 7,381 conflict-themed phishing URLs identified.

articles.title: The War Against Iran and Global Risks: 'Tell Me How This Ends'
articles.author: Widyane Hamdach
articles.summary: Examines the multidimensional consequences of the 2026 U.S.-Israeli military operation against Iran, arguing that without clear end objectives, the conflict risks becoming strategically overextended. Recommends prioritizing diplomacy and multilateral engagement over prolonged military involvement to prevent wider regional instability.
articles.relevance: Frames the 'no end in sight' scenario risk — critical for stress-testing business continuity assumptions and financial exposure models that depend on conflict duration.

articles.title: Where the American-Israeli War on Iran Leaves the Gulf Arabs
articles.author: Kaitlyn Hashem
articles.summary: Analyzes how GCC states are being forced into a more confrontational stance toward Iran, moving away from their traditional neutral/conciliatory posture. Argues this shift carries significant negative implications for long-term diplomatic and economic relations across the region.
articles.relevance: The GCC's hardening stance (now formalized in the March 26 joint statement) may prolong the conflict and reshape Gulf security architecture, with direct implications for regional market stability and institutional risk appetite.

items.bold_heading: CISA KEV Update — CVE-2026-33634 (Aqua Trivy Supply Chain Compromise)
items.text: CISA added CVE-2026-33634 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog on 26 March 2026. A threat actor ('TeamPCP') compromised Aqua Security's Trivy vulnerability scanner via a supply chain attack, injecting credential-stealing malware into official distribution channels (GitHub Actions, Docker Hub images, release binaries). Trivy versions v0.69.4 and associated actions published 19–20 March are affected. Affected sector: cross-sector (any organization using Trivy in CI/CD pipelines).
items.recommended_action: Audit all CI/CD pipelines for Trivy usage; pin to safe versions (v0.69.3 or earlier, trivy-action v0.35.0); rotate all secrets accessible to affected runner environments.

items.bold_heading: Unit 42 Threat Brief Updated (26 March) — conflict-themed phishing surge
items.text: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 updated its Iran threat brief on 26 March, identifying 7,381 conflict-themed phishing URLs spanning 1,881 unique hostnames. Recent activity includes financial fraud exploiting brands with 'Emirates' in the name, and crypto/investment scams using 'Dubai'-branded domains targeting UAE consumers. The Void Manticore/Handala wiper threat via Microsoft Intune remains active. Affected sector: financial services, consumer.
items.recommended_action: Update email gateway and web proxy blocklists with Unit 42's published IOCs; reinforce user awareness of conflict-themed lures; validate Intune/MDM access controls.

items.bold_heading: DDoS and APT campaigns against Gulf financial infrastructure (ongoing)
items.text: SHIELDNXT reports highly organized Iran-linked threat actors conducting DDoS campaigns, APT infiltration, spear-phishing targeting senior executives, and unauthorized access attempts against banking and financial services across Kuwait, UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar. Banking apps and government portals across the Gulf have experienced multi-day outages. Affected sector: Gulf banking and financial services.
items.recommended_action: Verify DDoS mitigation is active on all public-facing services; review executive account security; monitor for credential harvesting follow-on activity.

items.bold_heading: CrowdStrike — Hydro Kitten financial sector targeting signal
items.text: CrowdStrike reports that Hydro Kitten, an IRGC-linked APT group, has signalled plans to target the financial sector. While large-scale state-sponsored campaigns have not yet materialized, a surge in Iran-aligned hacktivist activity (60+ active groups) includes DDoS operations, defacements, and alleged disruptions. CrowdStrike advises critical infrastructure and financial sector firms to prepare for escalation from nuisance-level disruption into coordinated or destructive operations.

items.bold_heading: Unit 42 — Iran internet blackout constraining but not eliminating state cyber ops
items.text: Iran has surpassed its 27th consecutive day of near-complete internet blackout (1–4% connectivity since February 28). Unit 42 assesses this is mitigating near-term state-sponsored cyber operations but warns that restoration of connectivity — which could accompany any ceasefire or diplomatic progress — would likely trigger a surge in coordinated campaigns. Financial institutions remain high-probability spillover targets.

items.bold_heading: Mandiant M-Trends 2026 — faster attacker hand-offs, backup infrastructure targeting
items.text: Mandiant's M-Trends 2026 report (published 24 March) reveals that threat actors are completing access hand-offs in as little as 22 seconds, with rising dwell times and ransomware operators increasingly targeting backup infrastructure. While not Iran-specific, the accelerating operational tempo underscores the need for detection speed improvements, particularly for organizations already facing elevated state-sponsored threat activity.
Pakistan-hosted U.S.-Iran indirect talks — possible in-person meeting March 27–28
items.why_it_matters: If talks materialize, markets and Hormuz transit could shift rapidly, triggering oil price movement and altering conflict trajectory.

items.text: Iran internet connectivity restoration signals
items.why_it_matters: Iran has been at 1–4% internet since February 28; any restoration — potentially linked to diplomatic progress — would likely be followed by a surge in state-sponsored cyber operations targeting Gulf infrastructure.

items.text: April 6 energy strike deadline approaching
items.why_it_matters: Trump's 10-day pause on striking Iran's energy facilities expires April 6; market pricing will increasingly reflect expectations about whether the deadline holds or is extended.

source_confidence.note: No fresh KUNA updates found on grocery supply or school closures for the past 24 hours. Iraq border status based on limited sourcing.
Strategic cyber items are based on vendor assessments and general trend reports; no Gulf-specific CERT updates found for the past 24 hours.
